topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The memo sat on my desk, stark white against the beige laminate. Transfer to Logistics. It felt less like a career move and more like an eviction notice. Every time I looked at the name of the new department, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I started checking the locks on my office door, even though I knew the building security was robust. I kept glancing over my shoulder, convinced someone was watching the way I handled my coffee mug, or the way I tapped my pen. The humming of the fluorescent lights seemed amplified, a low, insidious buzz that whispered secrets I couldn't quite decipher. I started leaving my workstation unlocked for brief moments, just to see if anything would happen—a misplaced file, a sudden shadow moving down the hall. My hands shook slightly as I tried to pack up my personal belongings; the boxes felt heavy, like they were full of invisible surveillance equipment.

[story 2]

"Logistics," the manager had said, his tone annoyingly cheerful, as if discussing a change of office furniture. But I didn't hear the cheer. I heard static. From the moment I saw the transfer order, the world had narrowed to a tunnel vision of potential threat. I began rearranging my desk supplies obsessively, aligning the staplers in perfect, rigid rows. If the order was official, then the order had to be compromised. I spent hours watching the elevator, noting the patterns of the doors opening and closing, convinced that the sequence of movements held a coded message aimed directly at me. I started refusing to use the main breakroom, opting instead for the quiet solitude of my cubicle, listening intently for footsteps that weren't supposed to be there, the silence between the sounds feeling dangerously loud.

[story 3]

I kept running through the transfer details in my head, replaying every word until the edges of my thoughts blurred. It wasn't just a move; it was an extraction. I noticed subtle things: the way the water cooler always seemed to be positioned just slightly too close to the fire exit, the way certain colleagues avoided direct eye contact, their gazes darting toward the windows. My pulse quickened whenever I walked past the supply closet; it was a small space, dark and confining, and I felt an overwhelming urge to check if the door was truly closed, to confirm that the boundary hadn't dissolved. When HR finally called me in, I kept my voice level, unnaturally so, forcing a calm facade while internally, every nerve ending was screaming a warning. I just wanted to know what the destination held, because I had a dreadful feeling that whatever was waiting for me in Logistics wasn't going to be welcoming.
